children.label: Professional Behaviours and Communication
Demonstrates the personal and professional values and behaviours set out in Good Medical Practice
Demonstrates the personal and professional values and behaviours set out in Good Medical Practice
Communicates effectively with patients, their relatives and members of the multidisciplinary team with whom they work including being open and honest when things go wrong
Demonstrates effective communication skills
Practices effective interpersonal skills, emphasising empathy, compassion, courtesy and respect
Demonstrates effective interpersonal skills
Obtains valid consent following the associated legal and professional principles
Legal and ethical principles for obtaining informed consent in adults; Processes for patients who are unable to consent
Management and Professional and Regulatory Requirements
Explains employment law and the relevance of the working time regulations
Knowledge of employment law and its application to clinical practice
Works within local and national systems for clinical governance and data protection
Knowledge of principles of clinical governance and data protection
Understands equality and diversity legislation
Knowledge of principles of equality and diversity legislation
Team Working
Demonstrates appropriate clinical leadership behaviour in the workplace
Demonstrates effective team working
Demonstrates the importance of non-technical behaviour in the functioning of a successful team
Demonstrates effective non-technical skills
Safety and Quality Improvement
Describes quality improvement theories and methodologies
Knowledge of system of profound knowledge (SOPK) and model for improvement
Compares audit, research and quality improvement
Describes the different role for data for audit, research and quality improvement
Describes the common threats to patient safety in theatre and the perioperative period, and describes how these are minimised by day-to-day work routines
Factors that contribute to drug errors in anaesthesia and strategies to reduce them
Demonstrates the importance of the non-technical aspects of care such as situation awareness, task management, decision making and team working in anaesthetic practice
Demonstration of satisfactory non-technical skills in clinical performance exam
Describes the requirements and processes for raising concerns
Knowledge of how to raise concerns in the NHS
Explains and demonstrates duty of candour
Demonstration of the principles of the GMC's Professional Duty of Candour
Prescribes and administers drugs safely
Demonstrates safe prescribing practice
Safeguarding
Explains local procedures for safeguarding vulnerable children and adults
Knowledge of child protection regulations and what action must be taken when non-accidental injury is suspected
Discusses the principles of adult safeguarding; empowerment, prevention, proportionality, protection, partnership, accountability
Knowledge of principles of adult safeguarding
Complies with professional requirements and legal processes when obtaining consent from vulnerable patients
Legal and ethical considerations for determining mental capacity
Research and Managing Data
Describes essential statistical techniques used in research
Principles of study design
Outcome measures and the uncertainty in their definition
Principles of meta-analysis and evidence based medicine
Types of data and their representation
The normal distribution and other examples of parametric distributions
Descriptive statistics of central tendency and variability
Basic probability theory and the relationship to confidence values
The null hypothesis
Simple statistical tests for different types of data
Type I and type II errors
Perioperative Medicine and Health Promotion
Explains the patient, anaesthetic and surgical factors influencing patient outcomes
Integrated perioperative care pathways in primary and secondary care and patient outcomes
Organisational interventions which improve patient outcomes (eg care bundles, enhanced recovery programmes)
Effects of acute and chronic disease on patient outcomes after surgery
Implications of lifestyle factors such as smoking, alcohol intake and substance abuse on patient outcomes
Applies a structured approach to preoperative anaesthetic assessment of ASA 1-3 patients prior to surgery and recognises when further assessment and optimisation is required
Understands and explains the appropriate role of other specialties in preoperative optimisation
Preoperative investigations including indications for specific tests
Interprets common preoperative investigations
Risk assessment and stratification relevant to the provision of perioperative care
Emergency surgery vs elective surgery in terms of physiology, psychology and preparation
ASA and NCEPOD classifications; their implications in preparing for and planning anaesthesia and postoperative care
Implications of common co-existing diseases on anaesthesia and surgery including but not exclusively: obesity, diabetes, asthma, ischaemic heart disease, hypertension, rheumatoid disease, epilepsy
Explains the effect that co-existing disease, subsequent treatment and surgical procedures may have on the conduct of anaesthesia and plans perioperative management accordingly
Relevance of comorbid disease when planning and in the safe conduct of anaesthesia
Patient optimisation and risk reduction in the perioperative period

Management of drug therapy for co-existing disease in the perioperative period including, but not exclusively, diabetic treatment, steroids, anticoagulants, cardiovascular and respiratory medication, anti-convulsants
Current guidance on early surgical management of hip fractures and the necessary assessment for anaesthesia, timing of surgery and reasons for delay
Pathophysiology of obstructive sleep apnoea and its relevance to anaesthesia
Explains individualises options and risks of anaesthesia and pain management to patients
Synthesises relevant information to develop a safe anaesthetic plan taking the patient's wishes into consideration
Describes the importance of perioperative nutrition and fasting
Nutritional status on patient outcomes
Perioperative fluid and feeding regimes
Indications for preoperative fasting and appropriate regimens
Recognises and acts on the specific perioperative care requirements in frail and elderly patients and those with cognitive impairment
Anaesthetic implications for the following patient groups: the elderly, pregnant women, patients with cognitive impairment, patients with chronic pain and recreational drug users
Considers patient informed preference when obtaining consent for anaesthetic procedures
Principles of consent for surgery and anaesthesia, including the issue of capacity

Explains the guidance given by the GMC on consent, in particular: Understands that consent is a process that may culminate in, but is not limited to, the completion of a consent form; Understands the particular importance of considering the patient's level of understanding and mental state [and also that of the parents, relatives or carers when appropriate] and how this may impair their capacity for consent
Describes and recognises the role of socio-economic, environmental and lifestyle factors in health and illness
Demonstrates understanding of the relevance of socio-economic, environmental and lifestyle factors on outcomes from surgery
Describes and utilises appropriate antibiotic prophylaxis and prevention and treatment of infections
Types of hospital acquired infections and the precautions needed to reduce their transmission including prions
Concept of cross infection including: Modes of cross infection; Common cross infection agents
Explains the need for antibiotic policies in hospitals
The causes, prevention and treatment of infections seen in surgical, critically ill and immunocompromised patients
Discusses the need for, and application of, hospital immunisation policies
Principles and practice in using prophylactic antibiotics
Explains the principles and philosophy of antibiotic stewardship
Explains the environmental impact of healthcare and the principles of sustainable clinical practice
Demonstrates understanding of the environmental impact of anaesthesia
Applies local policies to prevent venous thromboembolism and understands the implications for anaesthetic practice on an individualised basis
Thromboprophylaxis in the perioperative period
Prevention of thromboembolic disease following surgery
Local/national guidelines on management of thrombo-embolic risk
Explains the specific perioperative care requirements of children including anxiety management
Knowledge of premedication for children
Explains the perioperative implications of pregnancy and initiates management of common serious diseases related to pregnancy
Understanding of the management of pregnancy related hypertension including pre-eclampsia
Describes the perioperative requirements for day surgery
Assesses patient suitability for day case admission
Factors determining suitability for treatment as an ambulant or day-stay patient
Principles of preoperative assessment and investigation of patients requiring day surgery including nurse-led assessment
Describes protocols for selection of day surgery patients including medical, surgical and social factors
Postoperative care specific to day case including discharge criteria, transport arrangements and when to drive
Causes of unanticipated in-patient admission following day surgery
Safely prescribes and administers blood products
Infections transmitted through contaminated blood including but not limited to: HIV, Hepatitis B and C
Understanding of methods of safe transfusion of blood and blood products
General Anaesthesia
Conducts comprehensive pre-anaesthetic and pre-operative checks
Describes the need for a targeted and relevant clinical examination
Describes the basis for clinical signs and the relevance of positive and negative physical signs
Anaesthetic history taking
Recognises that patients do not always present history in a structured fashion
Lists the likely causes and risk factors for conditions relevant to mode of presentation
Uses the patient's agenda and history to inform examination, investigation and management
Indications for rapid sequence induction
Specific complications of anaesthetic drugs including anaphylaxis, suxamethonium apnoea and malignant hyperpyrexia; prediction of patients who are at increased risk of these complications
Risk factors for post-operative nausea and vomiting

Obtains a history specifically relevant to the planned anaesthesia and surgery including: A history of the presenting complaint for surgery; A systematic comprehensive relevant medical history; Information about current and past medication; Drug allergy and intolerance; Information about previous anaesthetics and relevant family history
Performs a relevant clinical examination including when appropriate: Cardiovascular system; Respiratory system; Central and peripheral nervous system; Airway assessment
Factors that influence the risk of gastric reflux/aspiration and strategies to reduce it
Safely manages induction and maintenance of anaesthesia by inhalational and intravenous techniques, extubation and emergence from anaesthesia
Gives examples of methods of anaesthesia that are suitable for common operations
Indications for pre-medication

Makes appropriate plans for anaesthesia: Reviews current medication and seeks advice where appropriate; Plans appropriate anaesthetic technique[s]; Secures consent for anaesthesia; Recognises the need for additional investigation and acts accordingly; Discusses issues of concern with relevant members of the team
Explains the importance of maintaining the principles of aseptic practice and minimising the risks of hospital acquired infection
Principles of management of the airway including maintenance of a clear airway and the use of airway adjuncts and supraglottic airway devices
Advantages and disadvantages of intubation and supraglottic airway devices for airway management during anaesthesia
Different types of supraglottic devices

Indications for tracheal intubation; Types of tracheal tube and identifies their applications; Choice of the correct size and length of tracheal tube; Advantages/disadvantages of different types of laryngoscopes and blades including, but not exclusively, the Macintosh and McCoy and videolaryngoscope; Confirmation of correct placement of a tracheal tube; identification and complications of endobronchial or oesophageal intubation; Management of difficult intubation and failed intubation; Identification of patients who are at increased risk of regurgitation and pulmonary aspiration; measures that minimise the risk
Rapid sequence induction of anaesthesia
Indication and techniques for fibre-optic intubation
Problems associated with limb tourniquets and their safe use
Hazards associated with positioning – supine, lateral, prone, sitting
Anaesthesia for surgery in the prone and lateral positions
Blood conservation in major surgery
Surgical procedures for managing hip fractures, the anaesthetic requirements for each and the current evidence for the choice of anaesthetic technique

Pathophysiology, diagnosis and management of specific orthopaedic surgical complications that are relevant to anaesthesia including but not exclusively: Bone cement Implantation Syndrome; Diagnosis and management of fat embolism; Upper and lower limb compartment syndrome
Anaesthetic techniques appropriate for day cases including appropriate drugs
Pre-oxygenation, effects, limitations and correct techniques for its use
Intravenous and inhalational induction; techniques, advantages and disadvantages of both
Factors influencing the choice of agent for inhalational or intravenous induction of anaesthesia
Non anaesthetic physiological effects of intravenous and inhalational induction
Identifies the special problems of induction associated with cardiac disease, respiratory disease, musculoskeletal disease, obesity and those at risk of regurgitation/pulmonary aspiration
Removal of tracheal tubes and supraglottic airway devices and associated complications
Tracheal extubation strategies and management of laryngospasm at extubation
Physical and physiological effects of laparoscopic surgery including the effects of positioning (eg Trendelenberg, reverse Trendelenberg)
Plans recovery care, and manages recovery from anaesthesia utilising safe discharge criteria
Indications for, contra-indications to and complications of oxygen therapy in the post-operative period
Techniques for administration of oxygen therapy and performance characteristics of available devices
Identification and management of respiratory complications seen in recovery
Identification and management of cardiovascular complications seen in recovery
Identification and management of other common complications seen in recovery
Monitoring and the frequency of observations required for patients having undergone different types of surgery
Care of an unconscious patient in the recovery room, including safe positioning
Patient factors possibly requiring higher levels (level 2 or 3) of care in the postoperative period
Lists the appropriate discharge criteria for patients leaving the recovery room and for day stay patients
Explains the importance of following up patients in the ward after surgery
Principles of postoperative fluid regimes including volumes, types of fluid and monitoring of fluid balance
Diagnoses and manages common peri-operative complications
Contribution of PONV to post-operative outcomes and satisfaction
Predisposing factors for PONV
Regimes for prevention and treatment of PONV
Recognition and management of incomplete reversal of neuromuscular blockade
Causes and management of postoperative confusion
Causes and management of postoperative hypo and hypertension
Prevention, diagnosis and management of postoperative pulmonary atelectasis
Causes and management of stridor
Signs of pulmonary aspiration and its emergency management
Recognises anaesthetic critical incidents and explains their causes and management
Unexpected fall in SpO2
Unexpected increase in peak airway pressure
Progressive fall in minute volume during spontaneous respiration or IPPV
Fall in end tidal CO2
Rise in end tidal CO2
Rise in inspired CO2
Unexpected hypotension
Unexpected hypertension
Arrythmias
Convulsions
Regurgitation or aspiration of stomach contents
Laryngospasm
Difficulty with IPPV, sudden or progressive loss of minute volume
Bronchospasm
Pneumothorax and tension pneumothorax
Gas, fat and pulmonary embolism
Adverse drug reactions
Anaphylactic reactions and the appropriate management including follow up and patient information
Transfusion reactions (major and minor), management of inadvertent transfusion of mis-matched blood or blood products
Inadvertent intra-arterial injection of irritant fluids
High or total spinal block
Local anaesthetic toxicity
Accidental decannulation of tracheostomy or tracheal tube
Brain stem herniation due to increased intracranial pressure
Malignant hyperpyrexia
Awareness of the importance of critical incident reporting
The provision of information to the patient and where necessary ensuring that they get the appropriate counselling and advice with appropriate supervision
The need to follow through a critical incident with proper reporting, presentation at morbidity meetings and warning flags as necessary
Demonstrates knowledge of standard equipment used in anaesthetic practice with an understanding of relevant underpinning physics and clinical measurement involved
F1: Equipment
The functions and safety features of the anaesthetic machine
Patient warming systems: principles
Warming equipment for intravenous fluids: principles
Storage of gases and vapours including safety
Suction devices
Scavenging devices
Cylinders and pipelines, Bourdon gauge
Vaporisers: principles including plenum and draw over, temperature compensation, calibration
Electrical hazards: causes and prevention
Electrocution: including microshock, earth faults, leakage
Electrical equipment safety: medical, classification, types of equipment, symbols
ECG: principles including electrodes and electrode placement
Lasers: basic principles and safety
Cardiac pacemakers: Classification and safety
Defibrillators and defibrillation: principles including thoracic impedance, implantable devices
Diathermy: monopolar, bipolar; safety and uses
Plenum systems: warming blankets, theatre and anaesthetic room ventilation
Breathing systems: Mapleson classification, coaxial systems, circle systems, Tpiece, resuscitation breathing devices
Ventilators: principles including pressure and flow generators, cycling, jet and oscillator ventilators
Disconnections: monitoring of ventilator disconnection, warning devices
Fires and explosions: risks and prevention
Infusion pumps and syringe drivers; PCA devices, epidural infusion devices. Principles, use, safety and relevant drug calculations
Principles of hygiene including disinfection and sterilisation of equipment
Methods of decontamination, disinfection and sterilisation
Describes the benefits and limitations of technology and equipment in maintaining patient safety
F2: Physics
SI units: fundamental and derived units
SI and non SI units relevant to anaesthesia including mmHg, bar, atmospheres, cm H2O, PSI
Simple mechanics: mass, force, work, energy, power where these are relevant to anaesthetic practice
Heat: including temperature, absolute zero
Heat transfer and loss with relevance to clinical anaesthesia: conduction, convection, radiation and evaporation
Latent heat
Colligative properties: relevance of the concepts of osmolarity, osmolality and diffusion to anaesthetic practice
Physics of gases. Gas laws: kinetic theory of gases, Boyle's, Henry's, Dalton's, Charles' and Gay-Lussac's
Critical temperature and pressure
Physics of vapours
Pressure; absolute and relative pressure, gauge pressure
Principles of laminar and turbulent flow; Hagen-Poiseuille equation, Reynold's number
Relevance of gas density and viscosity. Examples including helium
Bernoulli principle
Venturi effect and entrainment devices
Vapour pressure; saturated vapour pressure
Vaporisation; process of vaporisation
Principles of surface tension
Capacitance, inductance
Ultrasound: basic principles of ultrasound
Physics relevant to optical fibres
Doppler effect: principle and clinical application
Resonance, damping, frequency response and their relevance to monitoring systems used in anaesthetic practice
F3: Clinical measurement
Basic measurement concepts relevant to understanding of monitoring in anaesthesia: linearity, drift, hysteresis, signal to noise ratio, static and dynamic response
Temperature measurement including mercury, alcohol, infrared, thermistor, thermocouple, Bourdon gauge, liquid crystal
Anatomical sites used for temperature measurement
Measurement of lung volumes and diffusion
Measurement of volume and flow in gases and liquids including pneumotachograph and other respirometers
Electrical interference; sources, methods of reduction
Pressure transducers
Capnography
Pulse oximetry
Measurement of gas pressures
Blood pressure: direct and indirect measurement including complications
Cardiac output: principles of measurement
Principles of measurement of pH, PCO2, PO2, electrolytes
Derived blood gas variables eg bicarbonate measures, base excess, oxygen consumption, respiratory quotient
Measurement of gas and vapour concentrations; including infrared, paramagnetic, fuel cell, oxygen electrode, mass spectrometry
Simple tests of pulmonary function: peak flow rate, spirometry
Assessment of neuromuscular blockade; quantitative and qualitative
Environmental monitoring; contamination by anaesthetic gases and vapours
Basic function and purpose of monitors
Minimum monitoring standards and indications for additional monitoring
Principles of calibration of monitoring equipment
Understanding the limits of monitoring equipment
CO2 absorption: chemistry and complications
Principles of measurement of parameters such as haemoglobin, blood glucose and coagulation using near-patient testing devices
Demonstrates knowledge of anatomy, physiology, biochemistry and pharmacology relevant to anaesthetic practice
G1: Anatomy
Mouth, nose, pharynx, larynx, trachea, main bronchi, segmental bronchi, structure of the bronchial tree; age related changes from the neonate to the adult
Airway/respiratory tract blood supply and innervation
Pleura, including surface anatomy, mediastinum and its contents
Lungs: lobes and microstructure of lungs
Diaphragm, other muscles of respiration including innervation
The thoracic inlet and first rib; interpretation of the normal adult chest X ray
Heart: chambers, valves, conducting system and pericardium, blood supply and innervation
Great vessels, main peripheral arteries and veins
Brain and its subdivisions; blood supply
Spinal cord, structure of spinal cord, major ascending and descending pathways and blood supply
Anatomical organisation of pain and sensory pathways from the periphery to the central nervous system
Pain pathways relevant to the stages of obstetric labour and delivery
Spinal meninges, subarachnoid and extradural space, contents of extradural space
Anatomy of CSF system
Spinal nerves; dermatomes and applied knowledge of dermatomes in regional anaesthesia
Brachial plexus; nerves of the upper limb
Intercostal nerves
Nerves of the abdominal wall including innervation of the inguinal region
Lumbar and sacral plexuses; nerves of the lower limb
Anatomical organisation of the autonomic nervous system
Sympathetic innervation, sympathetic chain, ganglia and plexuses
Parasympathetic innervation; cranial and sacral outflow
Stellate ganglion
Cranial nerves
Innervation of the pharynx and larynx
Eye and orbit
Functional anatomy of the hypothalamic/pituitary system
Functional anatomy of the adrenal gland
Functional anatomy of the thyroid and parathyroid glands
Cervical, thoracic and lumbar vertebrae
Sacrum, sacral hiatus
Ligaments of the vertebral column where relevant to anaesthetic practice
Surface anatomy of vertebral spaces; length of the spinal cord and subarachnoid space, age related differences from the neonate to the adult
Structures in the antecubital fossa
Structures in the axilla
Large veins of the neck. Anterior triangle of the neck: surface anatomy and ultrasound demonstrated anatomy relevant to insertion of central venous cannulae
Large veins of the leg; femoral triangle
Arteries of upper and lower limbs
Landmarks for performance of cricoid pressure and surgical airway procedures
Landmarks for the insertion of intercostal drainage catheters and needle decompression of pneumothorax
G2: Physiology
Organisation of the human body and the control of the internal environment
Changes with advancing age
Cells: components and organelles
Function of cells: genes and their expression
Cell membrane characteristics; cell junctions, receptors
Protective mechanisms of the body
Definition of pH, strong and weak acids
Acid base balance; buffers, Henderson Hasselbach equation and anion gap
Ions eg Na+, K+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Cl-, HCO3-
Cellular metabolism; aerobic vs anaerobic
Fetus: physiological changes at birth
Enzymes
Capillary dynamics and interstitial fluid; osmosis, filtration and convection
Osmolarity; osmolality, partition of fluids across membranes, tonicity
Lymphatic system
Special fluids: CSF, pleural, pericardial, peritoneal
Active cellular transport mechanisms
Blood: physical properties, components, functions
Red blood cells: production and turnover, haematinics, haemoglobin and its variant including abnormal haemoglobins
Anaemia; acute and chronic adaptations, iron absorption, transportation and metabolism
Polycythaemia: causes and implications
Blood groups: ABO, Rhesus, others
Transfusion reactions; Rhesus incompatibility
Haemostasis and coagulation, fibrinolysis. Abnormalities, congenital and acquired
Alternative oxygen carrying solutions
White blood cells: types, origins, characteristics, turnover

The inflammatory response, systemic inflammatory responses, hypersensitivity reactions. Immunity and allergy: innate vs acquired, non-specific vs specific, humoral vs cellular. Immunodeficiency – congenital and acquired
Action potential generation and transmission
Neuromuscular junction and transmission, motor end-plate
Disturbances of neuromuscular transmission
Myopathies, congenital and acquired
Muscle contracture: malignant hyperthermia, myoclonus, burns
Muscle types: skeletal, smooth, cardiac
Skeletal muscle excitation-contraction coupling
Smooth muscle contraction; sphincters
Motor unit concept
Cardiac muscle contraction
Cardiac cycle: pressure volume relationships, work and power
Rhythmicity of the heart; cardiac impulse generation
Regulation of cardiac function; general and cellular
Control of cardiac output; Starling relationship
Fluid challenge and heart failure, types of shock
Electrocardiogram and arrhythmias, origin of ECG, effects of temperature, ischaemia, infarction and electrolyte imbalance
Neurological and humoral control of systemic blood pressure, blood volume and blood flow; at rest and during physiological disturbance eg exercise, haemorrhage and Valsalva manoeuvre
Peripheral circulation: capillaries, vascular endothelium and arteriolar smooth muscle
Functions of endothelium
Characteristics of special circulations including pulmonary, coronary, cerebral, renal, portal, transitional and fetal
Structure and function of renal circulation
Blood flow and glomerular filtration, plasma clearance and tubule-glomerular feedback
Tubular function and urine formation; transport processes in kidney
Assessment of renal function
Regulation of water and electrolyte balance; response to fluid loss/hypovolaemia
Role of urea and creatinine measurement
Regulation of acid-base balance
Pathophysiology of acute kidney injury and renal failure
Gaseous exchange: O2 and CO2 transport, hypoxia and hyper/hypocapnia, hyper and hypobaric pressures
Function of haemoglobin in oxygen carriage and acid-base equilibrium
Pulmonary ventilation: volumes, capacities, flows, dead space, compliance, work of breathing
Effect of IPPV on lungs
Mechanics of ventilation; ventilation/perfusion abnormalities, regional V/Q, surfactant
Control of breathing, acute and chronic ventilator failure, effect of oxygen therapy
Effects of altitude
Non-respiratory functions of the lungs
Neuronal structure and function, resting membrane potential, action potentials, conduction, synaptic mechanisms, actions of neurotransmitters
The brain; functional divisions
Brain stem: organisation, interconnections. Intracranial pressure, CSF and blood flow
Autonomic nervous system: organisation, ganglia, adrenergic vs cholinergic
Neurological reflexes: monosynaptic, polysynaptic, stretch, inhibition
Motor function: basal ganglia, spinal and peripheral; Sense: receptors, nociception, proprioception, sight, taste, smell, hearing, balance, touch, temperature

Pain: afferent nociceptive pathways, dorsal horn, peripheral and central mechanisms, neuromodulatory systems, supraspinal mechanisms, visceral pain, neuropathic pain, influence of therapy on nociceptive mechanisms
Physiology of nausea and vomiting
Metabolic and digestive functions
Gastric function: motility, secretions, nausea and vomiting
Digestive functions: composition of secretions, digestion of carbohydrates, lipids, proteins. Energy homeostasis
Body mass/composition. Body mass index, body fat estimation
Principles of nutrition: carbohydrates, fats, proteins, vitamins and minerals
Energy requirements/expenditure and measurement
Metabolic pathways, energy production and enzymes, metabolic rate, lactate metabolism
Hormonal control of metabolism: regulation of plasma glucose, response to trauma
Physiological alterations in starvation, exercise and the stress response
Body temperature and its regulation including at extremes of age
Hormones: types, receptors, hierarchy, extracellular signalling
Mechanisms of hormonal control: feedback mechanisms, effects on membrane and intracellular receptors
Hypothalamic and pituitary function
Adrenocortical hormones
Adrenal medulla: adrenaline and noradrenaline
Pancreas: insulin, glucagons and exocrine function
Thyroid and parathyroid hormones and calcium homeostasis
Physiological changes associated with pregnancy
Materno-fetal, fetal and neonatal circulation
Functions of the placenta; placental transfer
G3: Biochemistry and pharmacology
Exponential functions including wash-in, wash-out where relevant to anaesthetic practice
Logarithms relevant to anaesthetic or critical care practice
Area under the curve (integration) and rate of change (differentiation) relevant to anaesthetic practice

Organic chemistry: drugs as organic molecules, types of intermolecular bonds, interactions between molecules, organic compared with inorganic compounds, bond strength, important atomic constituents, C, N, O, P, S and halides
Organic chemistry: ionisation of molecules, types of group that ionise, amides, hydroxyl, carboxyl
Oxidation and reduction
Permanently charged (quaternary ammonium) drugs
Drug chemistry: solubility, partition coefficients and movement of drugs through membranes, lipid solubility, influence of pKa and pH
Passive and active transport mechanisms
Isomers: structural and stereo, classification systems, clinical relevance
Mechanisms of drug action: physicochemical, pharmacodynamics, pharmacokinetic, drug-receptor interactions, dose response and log doseresponse curves, agonists, partial agonists, antagonists
Reversible and irreversible antagonism. Potency and efficacy
Non-specific drug actions; physic chemical mechanisms eg adsorption, chelation, neutralisation
Voltage-gated ion channels, membrane bound transport pumps
Sodium, potassium and calcium channels as targets for drug action
Receptors as proteins, ion channels, transmembrane transduction and intermediate messenger systems, intracellular/nuclear receptors
Receptor regulation and tachyphylaxis
Response time of ligand gated receptor interaction, enzymes as drug targets, Michaelis-Menten kinetics
Direct and allosteric mechanisms eg acetylcholinesterase, cyclo-oxygenase, phosphodiesterase
Anticholinesterases; classification of drugs that inhibit acetylcholinesterase and plasma cholinesterase including organophosphates
Predictable side effects of drugs, non selective actions of drugs, action at multiple receptors, multiple anatomical locations, predictable enzyme induction/inhibition
Idiosyncratic side effects of drugs eg blood and bone marrow dyscrasias, pulmonary fibrosis, antiplatelet effects
Anaphylactic and anaphylactoid reactions; comparison, treatment, identification of responsible drug, risks of polypharmacy
Tachyphylaxis and tolerance; examples of drugs demonstrating tachyphylaxis, proposed mechanisms
Drug interactions: types of interaction, synergism, additivity, antagonism, isobolograms
Classification of mechanisms of drug interactions

Pharmacokinetics: general principles – absorption, distribution and redistribution, elimination, excretion. Chemical properties of drugs and their pharmacokinetics. Blood-brain barrier and placental barrier. Protein binding – plasma and tissue. Body compartments, adipose and vessel poor tissue. Bioavailability, clearance

Administration and absorption; routes of administration; first pass metabolism and bioavailability. Selection of appropriate route. Drug delivery systems eg sustained release, enteric coated, transdermal patch and iontophoretic systems
Oral administration: time course for systemic appearance, factors eg pKa, lipid solubility, active transport
Bioavailability of drugs given orally and its measurement
Drug elimination from plasma. Mechanisms: distribution, metabolism, excretion, exhalation, renal, biliary, sweat, breast milk
Factors affecting drug elimination eg pathological state, renal and hepatic failure, age, gender, drug interactions
Active and inactive metabolites, pro-drugs. Enzyme induction and inhibition
Non-enzymatic drug elimination. Hofmann degradation
Pharmacokinetic modelling: types of models available, one, two and three compartment models, non-compartmental, physiological
Pharmacokinetic parameters: volume of distribution, half-life and time constant, clearance
Context sensitive half-time: comparison of drugs eg propofol, fentanyl and remifentanil. Target controlled infusions (TCI)
TCI in practice: accuracy, applicability, cost. Variations due to patient differences – predictable and unpredictable
Pharmacogenetics: pharmacokinetic variation eg pseudocholinesterase, acetylation, CYP450 variants
Differences in patient response to therapy: gender, pathology, polypharmacy, changes occurring with increasing age
Poor and fast metabolisers, racial and geographic distribution of common abnormal genes
Volatile and gaseous anaesthetic agents: structure of available agents, MAC, clinical effects on CNS, CVS, RS, pharmacokinetics including metabolism
Mechanisms of general anaesthetic action

Factors that affect the onset and offset time. Oil/gas partition coefficient. Intravenous anaesthetic agents: chemical classes, properties of an ideal induction agent, adverse effects on CNS, CVS, RS, pharmacokinetics including metabolism
Benzodiazepines: classification of action, clinical actions, synergism with anaesthetic agents, antidote in overdose

Local anaesthetic agents. Additional effects including anti-arrhythmic effects. Mechanism of action. Clinical factors influencing choice: operative site, patient, available agents. Toxicity syndrome: safe clinical and maximum clinical doses, treatment of overdose
Analgesics. Simple analgesics, NSAIDs and opioids. Available routes of administration, perioperative prescribing, chronic compared with acute pain prescribing
Aspirin and paracetamol. Comparison of structures, indications and contraindications, mechanism of action, bioavailability, metabolism, toxicity
Non steroidal anti inflammatory analgesics: classification, mechanism of action, clinical effects and uses, unwanted effects, contraindications

Opioid analgesics: receptor classification, mechanism of action, inhibitory effects, sites of action on pain pathways, unwanted effects, full and partial agonists, routes of administration. Opioid dependence and tolerance

Muscle relaxants: Classification. Sites of action. Properties of an ideal muscle relaxant. Dantrolene and management of MH. Depolarizing muscle relaxants: Structure, mechanism of action. Organophosphate poisoning. Adverse effects and contraindications

Non-depolarising muscle relaxants: structural classification, sub-classification according to onset time and duration of action. Comparison of aminosteroids and benzylisoquinoliniums. Comparison of individual agents: metabolism and active metabolites. Unwanted effects
Reversal of neuromuscular blockade: indications for use, mechanism of action, clinically unwanted effects of reversal of neuromuscular blockade
Drugs and the autonomic nervous system: anatomy, myelinated and unmyelinated nerves, ganglia and rami communicantes
Neurotransmitters: sites at which drugs act to interfere with autonomic transmission

Drugs and the sympathetic nervous system: adrenergic receptors and molecular mechanisms of action, indications for pharmacological use of naturally occurring and synthetic analogues. Other classes of drugs active in the sympathetic system eg MAOIs
Drugs and the parasympathetic nervous system: nicotinic and muscarinic receptors with subgroups, mechanism of action agonists and antagonists, comparison of available drugs
Cardiovascular system: drug effects on the heart and on the circulation, arterial and venous effects, systemic and pulmonary effects
Inotropes and pressors: classification, site of action, synthetic inotropes compared with adrenaline
Drugs used in ischaemic heart disease: classification of drugs uses, mechanism of drug action, use in unstable angina
Antiarrhythmic drugs: classification, indications for use including in resuscitation

Hypotensive agents: classes of drugs to produce acute hypotension in theatre. Therapeutic antihypertensive agents: classification according to mechanism of action. Adverse effects of drugs in each class

Anticoagulants: oral and parenteral. Sites of action, indications for use, monitoring effect. Comparison of heparins: unfractionated and fractionated. Newer anticoagulants. Antiplatelet agents. Perioperative management of antiplatelet and anticoagulant medication
Pro-coagulants: drugs, individual factor concentrates, multifactor preparations including FFP, vitamin K
Colloids including blood and blood products: composition of preparations, safe use and avoidance of errors
Crystalloid fluids: composition, suitable fluids for maintenance and replacement of losses. Comparison with colloids, unwanted effects
Electrolyte solutions including conversion between units eg molar, mg/ml, %

Respiratory system: classes of drug acting on the respiratory tract including bronchodilators, oxygen, surfactant, mucolytics, pulmonary vasodilators. Methods of administration, indications for use, mechanism of action, adverse effects
Respiratory system: drugs used in acute severe asthma and chronic asthma, volatile agents, mechanisms of action
Gastrointestinal system: antisialogogues, drugs reducing gastric acidity, drug effects on the GI tract including gastric and bowel motility
